panels.title: Waiting Clients
panels.description: Clients queued for a server connection. Sustained >0 means pool_size is insufficient — increase pool_size or reduce query duration.

panels.title: Wait p99
panels.description: 99th percentile client checkout wait. Above 50 ms: pool_size is the bottleneck before PostgreSQL is — raise it or shorten queries.

panels.title: Query p99
panels.description: 99th percentile server-side query time (excludes queue wait). Spike without QPS increase — check pg_stat_activity for locks or vacuum.

panels.title: Pool Utilization
panels.description: Active server connections / pool_size. Above 70%: anticipate saturation. Above 90%: clients are queuing.

panels.title: Memory
panels.description: Process RSS. Sudden growth without new connections usually means unbounded prepared statement cache — check Pool Cache Entries.

panels.title: Total Connections
panels.description: Cumulative client connections accepted (all pools). Compare with pool_size for multiplexing ratio — 100:1+ is normal in transaction mode.

panels.title: Clients by State
panels.description: Active (has server), idle (between transactions), waiting (queued). Growing 'waiting' area means pool_size is the bottleneck.

panels.title: Waiting Clients
panels.description: Waiting clients by user@database. Pinpoints which pool needs pool_size increase or query optimization.

targets.expr: histogram_quantile(0.95, sum by (le, user, database) (rate(pg_doorman_pools_wait_duration_seconds_bucket{instance=~"$instance", user=~"$user", database=~"$database"}[$__rate_interval])))

panels.title: Wait p95 by Pool
panels.description: 95th percentile client checkout wait per pool. The pool with the highest line is the one that needs pool_size attention.

panels.title: Servers by State
panels.description: Backend connections: active (executing query), idle (available for checkout). Idle approaching zero means no headroom for bursts.

panels.title: Pool Size vs Active Servers
panels.description: Active servers overlaid with pool_size ceiling. Gap between lines is spare capacity. When they converge, clients start queuing.

targets.expr: pg_doorman_pools_servers{status="active", instance=~"$instance", user=~"$user", database=~"$database"} / pg_doorman_pool_size{instance=~"$instance", user=~"$user", database=~"$database"} * 100

panels.title: Pool Utilization %
panels.description: Active/pool_size ratio over time. Sustained above 70% warrants pool_size increase; above 90% clients are already waiting.

panels.title: Query Latency Percentiles
panels.description: Server-side query time at p50/p90/p95/p99 from the per-pool histogram. p99 diverging from p50 — check pg_stat_activity for lock waits or long-running queries.

panels.title: Queries per Second
panels.description: Query throughput per pool. Flat QPS with rising latency signals PostgreSQL saturation. Rising QPS with stable latency is healthy growth.

panels.title: Transaction Latency Percentiles
panels.description: End-to-end transaction time at p50/p90/p95/p99 from the per-pool histogram. High values with low query latency indicate application-side delays between queries.

panels.title: Transactions per Second
panels.description: Transaction throughput per pool. Drop with rising latency indicates lock contention or long transactions holding server connections.

panels.title: Bytes Received
panels.description: Data rate from clients. Spikes correlate with bulk INSERTs/COPYs.

panels.title: Bytes Sent
panels.description: Data rate to clients. Large spikes indicate fat result sets — consider LIMIT if unexpected.

panels.title: Connections vs Max
panels.description: Total backend connections across all user pools vs max_db_connections. When current approaches max, coordinator evicts idle connections from lower-priority pools.

panels.title: Reserve Pool
panels.description: Reserve connections activated when all max_db_connections slots are full. Any usage means primary capacity exhausted — raise max_db_connections.

panels.title: Coordinator Events
panels.description: Evictions: idle connections reclaimed across pools (normal). Exhaustions: client errors, no connection available (critical — increase capacity).

panels.title: Inflight Creates
panels.description: Connections mid-handshake (TCP + auth + startup). Sustained high count — PostgreSQL slow to accept, check auth method or backend CPU.

panels.title: Scaling Events
panels.description: creates/s: new connections. gate_waits/s: throttled by burst limiter. fallback/s: anticipation missed, created on-demand.

panels.title: Connections by Type
panels.description: Connection rate per second by protocol. Track TLS adoption. Elevated cancel rate indicates application timeouts.

panels.title: Pool Cache Entries
panels.description: Unique prepared statements per pool. Unbounded growth means dynamic statement names — fix the app or cap with prepared_statements_cache_size.

panels.title: Cache Memory (Pool + Client)
panels.description: Memory in prepared caches (pool + client). When this dominates total memory, reduce cache size or fix dynamic statement names.

targets.expr: clamp_max(sum by (user, database) (pg_doorman_servers_prepared_hits{instance=~"$instance", user=~"$user", database=~"$database"}) / clamp_min(sum by (user, database) (pg_doorman_servers_prepared_hits{instance=~"$instance", user=~"$user", database=~"$database"}) + sum by (user, database) (pg_doorman_servers_prepared_misses{instance=~"$instance", user=~"$user", database=~"$database"}), 1), 1)

panels.title: Prepared Statement Hit Ratio
panels.description: Cache hits / total lookups. Below 90%: servers frequently re-parse after multiplexing. Ensure consistent statement names.

panels.title: Client Named Entries
panels.description: Sum of Named entries across all clients in the pool. Named is unbounded — drivers that mint per-query named statements (some pgjdbc / Hibernate / Npgsql configurations) drive this up without limit. Application is responsible for DEALLOCATE or name reuse.

panels.title: Client Anonymous Entries
panels.description: Sum of Anonymous entries across all clients. Bounded per client by client_anonymous_prepared_cache_size (default 256). Approaches at most connected_clients * cache_size.

panels.title: Anonymous LRU Eviction Rate
panels.description: Rate of evictions on the per-client Anonymous LRU. Sustained non-zero rate means client_anonymous_prepared_cache_size is too small for the workload, or the application generates unique queries on the hot path. Alert template: > 10/s for 10m.

panels.title: Auth Cache Hit Rate
panels.description: Auth lookups served from cache vs PostgreSQL query. Low rate adds latency to every new connection — increase cache_ttl.

panels.title: Auth Outcomes
panels.description: Auth success vs failure rate. Failure spike after deploy = credential mismatch. Sustained failures = check source IPs in logs.

panels.title: Dynamic Pools
panels.description: Auto-created pools for auth_query users. Unexpected growth indicates wrong database names or unplanned user sprawl.

panels.title: Process Memory
panels.description: Process RSS over time. Correlate with Total Connections and Cache Memory to isolate growth driver.

panels.title: Sockets by Type
panels.description: Open sockets by protocol. Count growing faster than connections indicates FD leak — check CLOSE_WAIT via SHOW SOCKETS.

panels.title: Local backend in cooldown
panels.description: 1 if any pool is currently using a fallback host. Sustained = local backend not recovering.

panels.title: Fallback Connections
panels.description: Connections routed to fallback hosts in the current window.

panels.title: Patroni API Errors
panels.description: Failed /cluster requests (all Patroni URLs unreachable).

panels.title: Patroni API Rate
panels.description: Patroni API calls, fallback connections, and errors per second. Errors without connections = all Patroni URLs or all candidates unreachable.

panels.title: Patroni API Duration
panels.description: Time to fetch /cluster from Patroni API. p99 above 1s = network issues or overloaded Patroni nodes.

panels.title: Fallback Cache Hits
panels.description: Fallback host served from cache without querying Patroni API. High rate during cooldown = cache working correctly.

panels.title: Synthetic SQLSTATE 26000 Rate
panels.description: Bind referencing an anonymous prepared whose text is no longer in any cache. Flat zero is the normal case. Sustained > 1/s = TTL too short for the workload, or a driver depending on cross-batch unnamed prepared statements.

panels.title: Interner Bytes by Kind
panels.description: Total length of interned Parse text per kind. ANON > 1.5 GiB is the alert threshold for the bundled prometheus rule (PgDoormanAnonInternerMemoryHigh).

panels.title: Interner Entries by Kind
panels.description: Live entries in the global query interner. NAMED is bounded by passive Arc::strong_count GC; ANON is bounded by query_interner_anon_idle_ttl_seconds. Sustained growth on either line points to either a long TTL on a unique-anon workload or an Arc<str> leak on the named side.

panels.title: Interner Eviction Rate
panels.description: Evictions per second, split by kind (named|anonymous) and reason (gc_passive for named, ttl_expired for anonymous). Steady ANON eviction is normal; steady NAMED eviction is unusual unless a flood of unique named statements just landed.

panels.title: GC Sweep Duration
panels.description: Wall-clock time of one GC sweep cycle (named + anonymous combined). P99 above 50 ms means the sweep is starting to bite into request latency tails — increase query_interner_gc_interval_seconds or shrink the interner via RESET INTERNER plus cache-size tuning.

panels.title: Paused Pools
panels.description: 1 when the pool is currently paused via PAUSE admin command, 0 when running. A pool stuck at 1 after incident triage drops all client traffic until manually resumed.

panels.title: Pool Max Wait (worst client)
panels.description: Largest single client checkout wait in each pool, taken as max(client.max_wait_time) across alive clients. Each client tracks its own lifetime maximum, so a spike means 'someone in this pool ever waited this long', not 'someone is waiting now'.

panels.title: Pool Errors per Second by SQLSTATE Class
panels.description: Backend errors per pool, bucketed by SQLSTATE class: 08 (connection_exception), 53 (insufficient_resources), 57 (operator_intervention), 25P02 (in_failed_sql_transaction), 26000 (invalid_sql_statement_name), other. The full 5-character code is in /api/pools and the Web UI.

panels.title: Pool Errors per Second by Pool
panels.description: Same counter aggregated per pool. Use this view to find which pool produces the bulk of errors when the SQLSTATE breakdown shows a spike.

panels.title: Pre-auth Rejections per Second by Reason
panels.description: Clients dropped before authentication, by reason: hba (HBA denied), tls_required (only_ssl_connections rejected plain text), tls_handshake_fail (TLS negotiation failed), protocol_error (unexpected startup sequence), invalid_startup (bad startup or socket error), too_many_clients (listener at capacity). Sustained 'hba' or 'tls_handshake_fail' is the bruteforce-from-outside signal.

panels.title: Streaming Events per Second
panels.description: Large messages forwarded byte-for-byte by pg_doorman. kind is data_row or copy_data; result is ok or error. Sustained non-zero rate signals oversized BYTEA/JSONB payloads, COPY rows with pathological content, or a misbehaving ORM — pg_doorman buffers most messages in RAM, but anything above max_message_size is streamed to keep memory bounded.

panels.title: Streaming Bytes per Second
panels.description: Bytes pushed through the streaming path (header + payload). Counted even on failed events, so this measures what actually reached the client wire, not only fully delivered messages.

panels.title: Backend Setup p99 by Phase
panels.description: 99th percentile of each backend connection setup phase: tcp_connect (raw socket), tls (SSL request + handshake), auth (StartupMessage to AuthenticationOK), startup (AuthenticationOK to ReadyForQuery). The phase you don't see is the phase that failed before completing.

panels.title: Backend Setup p50 by Phase
panels.description: Median of each setup phase. Compare against p99 to spot tail-latency outliers vs steady slowness.

panels.title: Backend Setup Rate by Phase
panels.description: Backend connections completing each phase per second. tcp_connect rate equals total backend creates; gaps to tls/auth/startup mark drop-offs at each step.
